finding: applyStreamFrame's 'complete' branch runs its state-clearing set() (isStreaming/sending/streamingMessage=null + deletes streamingMessage from the Map) unconditionally, before the isOnOriginalConversation check, unlike the 'started'/'content' branches which early-return on `get().conversation?.id !== conversationId` (lines 1319/1379); a complete frame for a conversation this store is not showing therefore wipes its streaming state.

closePane truncates dividerWidths from the END only (`length = panes.length-1`) instead of splicing at the removed index, so closing a non-last pane leaves surviving panes pinned to the wrong neighbour's width (dividerWidths[i] is meant to be pane i's width and the indices shift on splice).

Because every pane's `on('chat:token')` handler receives every pane's stream-client frames over the shared global EventBus, the 'error' branch's mismatch path (`get().conversation?.id !== conversationId` -> set isStreaming/sending/streamingMessage=null + clearConversationCache) fires for sibling panes: one pane's turn erroring clears a different pane's concurrently in-flight streaming state; the preceding onStreamError()/handleSSEEvent() at 1575-1577 are likewise invoked pane-agnostically.

SplitDivider adds window pointermove/pointerup listeners in onDown but removes them only in onUp; there is no useEffect unmount cleanup, so if the divider unmounts mid-drag (a pane is closed/reordered during a resize) the window listeners leak and onMove keeps calling setDividerWidth with the now-stale leftPaneIndex.

The raw-extension tail of applyStreamFrame calls chatExtensionRegistry.handleSSEEvent(sseEvent, get, set) with NO conversationId guard, and since each pane's client emits chat:token on the shared global EventBus every pane's handler runs it; extension events (titleUpdated, mcpToolStart/Complete/Progress, mcpApprovalRequired, artifactCreated) for one pane's conversation are thus applied to every pane's store, leaking title/tool-call/approval state across panes.

selectedByConversation (and AssistantPicker's identical map) is keyed by conversation id and never pruned on conversation delete — Chat.store's sync:conversation delete handler clears only its own conversationStateCache — so these maps grow unbounded and retain stale per-conversation model/assistant selections across the session; SplitView.persist likewise stores panes whose conversationId may since have been deleted.

focusedApi() reads useSplitViewStore.getState().focusedPaneId non-reactively; an outside-pane reactive `Stores.Chat.<field>` consumer (no PaneApiContext) binds useStore to the focused pane's api at render time and, because focusedPaneId lives in a different store, never re-renders or re-subscribes when focus moves to another pane, so it can show stale data from the previously-focused pane.

In split view every pane store subscribes to the GLOBAL chat:token bus and runs applyStreamFrame for EVERY pane's frames, but the default extension branch dispatches handleSSEEvent(get,set) with no conversation-id guard, so a titleUpdated/mcp/artifact event for pane A's conversation is applied to pane B's store (e.g. the title extension blindly overwrites get().conversation.title), bleeding conversation A's derived title into pane B's view.

The 'error' branch calls onStreamError + handleSSEEvent unconditionally and then, on a conversation-id MISMATCH, set({isStreaming:false,sending:false,streamingMessage:null,...}) — so an error frame for pane A's conversation (delivered via the global chat:token bus) wipes pane B's OWN in-flight streaming state, breaking the sibling pane's live generation.

The web pop-out opens window.open(`/chat/${conversationId}`) with only the conversation UUID in the URL and no token — the fresh SPA self-authenticates on boot, so no access/refresh token is leaked via the popout URL (no material issue).

Capability 'windows' widened from ['main'] to ['main','chat-*'], granting the default permission set (core:default/opener:default/etc.) to any webview labeled chat-*; labels are derived as chat-<conversation-uuid> so the wildcard is scoped and grants no new permission, but the glob would also match any future non-conversation chat-* window.

register() still enforces BOTH global and per-user caps, now reading the configured inner.limits.per_user_max_connections instead of the hardcoded 12; the 429 rejection paths are preserved and no authorization gate changed — the only behavioral change is the default cap raised 12->24 (DEC-34), verified by the added per_user_cap_honors_the_configured_limit test.

ChatConfig performs no validation that per_user_max_connections <= global_max_connections or that either is non-zero; a misconfigured per_user>global or 0 is honored, but register() checks each cap independently and fails closed (429), so this is a robustness gap, not a cap-bypass.

MAX_PANES=3 is a client-side ergonomics cap only; the real resource bound (SSE connections) is enforced server-side per-user, and pop-out windows/tabs each add one connection, all still bounded by the server's per-user cap (now 24) — no client-side cap bypasses the server limit.

The composeRequestFields(ctx: ChatHookCtx) signature is threaded consistently: registry passes ctx to each extension, types.ts declares the optional-arg contract, and the two conversation-keyed implementers (assistant + user-llm-providers/model) consume ctx.conversationId while file/text/mcp implementers safely ignore it; all handleSSEEvent(get,set) call sites and the events.ts fallback were updated — contract is internally consistent.

The load-bearing comment 'applyStreamFrame filters by conversation id, so a frame for another pane's conversation is a cheap no-op here' is inaccurate: only the 'started' and 'content' branches guard on conversation?.id===conversationId; the 'complete', 'error', and default extension branches run side effects (handleSSEEvent / streaming-state resets) BEFORE any guard, so the documented per-pane isolation contract does not match the actual behavior.

The per-user default 24 is duplicated in two sources of truth — ChatStreamLimits::default() (registry.rs:39) and default_chat_per_user_max_connections() (config.rs:345) — with no cross-check; since apply_config_limits always overrides at boot they agree today, but a future change to one and not the other would silently diverge for the pre-boot/test window.

Stale comment references 'the server's per-user 12-connection cap' and the const name PER_USER_MAX_CONNECTIONS, but this branch renamed that to ChatStreamLimits and raised the default to 24 — the frontend doc now misstates the backend contract it cites.

putSubscription awaits fetch() but never checks response.ok, so a non-2xx PUT (stale connection-id 4xx, a 429 under the newly-raised per-user cap, or a 401) resolves successfully and is silently swallowed — the pane keeps receiving no tokens until an unrelated reconnect handshake re-PUTs; only network throws are caught/warned.

SplitDivider registers window pointermove/pointerup listeners on pointer-down and removes them only in onUp; there is no useEffect unmount cleanup, so if the divider unmounts mid-drag (a pane closes) the listeners leak and onMove keeps calling setDividerWidth with a now-stale leftPaneIndex.

loadConversation/sendMessage/reset call get().chatStreamClient?.setActiveConversation(...) with optional chaining, so if invoked before init created the client the subscription silently no-ops with no warning; correctness relies entirely on init running first, with no guard or log if it hasn't.

ModelSelector destructures the whole selectedByConversation map reactively and the immer store replaces that object's identity on every set, so picking a model in one split pane re-renders every pane's ModelSelector rather than only the changed pane.

AssistantStatusChip (and AssistantSelector.tsx:13, AssistantMenuItem.tsx:13) now read the entire selectedByConversation map reactively from the immer-backed AssistantPicker store, so any pane's assistant change invalidates the map identity and re-renders all panes' assistant controls instead of just the one that changed.

Each pane subscribes its own chat:token handler on the shared EventBus, so every streamed token frame fans out to all N panes' applyStreamFrame (N-1 filtered as cheap no-ops per frame) — O(panes) invocations per token rather than routing the frame only to the owning pane.

The divider drag uses physical e.clientX and getBoundingClientRect().width and pins the 'left' pane width, while the surrounding layout uses logical CSS props (end-0, border-s, inset-y-0); the pointer math is not logical-direction-consistent, so under RTL the resize direction inverts.

Stores.Chat is registered as a hand-rolled UseBoundStore-shaped facade that calls useContext/useStore under eslint-disable react-hooks/rules-of-hooks rather than a defineStore product; it deviates from the store-kit idiom (deliberate/documented, but a new non-standard store shape the module proxy must tolerate).

The re-key keeps providers/availableAssistants as global defineStore singleton catalogs and moves only the selection into a per-conversation map, so the model/assistant catalog still lazy-loads exactly once across all panes (no per-pane catalog reload) — the ModelPicker/AssistantPicker re-key is conformant on the load-once concern.

The resize divider (role="separator") is drag-only via onPointerDown with no tabIndex, no keydown handler, and no aria-valuenow/valuemin/valuemax, so keyboard-only users cannot resize panes (WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard) and AT gets no current-size feedback.

The in-pane right-panel slide-over is a bare div (z-50) with no role="dialog"/region, no aria-label, no Escape-to-close, and no focus trap, yet the comment claims it mirrors the narrow Drawer which explicitly provides focus-trap/Escape — so this overlay is not announced as a region and cannot be dismissed by keyboard.

The focused pane is indicated only visually (ring-2 ring-primary) and the pane container has no role/aria-label/aria-current, so screen-reader users cannot tell which of the N panes is focused or distinguish one pane from another.

headerControls (find toggle, split button, open-in-new-window) render with identical aria-labels in every pane header, so in a multi-pane split AT users hear the same button names repeated with no pane-scoping context to tell which pane each acts on.

The new SplitView store's pure, high-value logic (MAX_PANES cap returning null, the atomic closePane focus-reassignment flagged as a Round-2 finding, setDividerWidth MIN/MAX clamp, reorderPanes bounds) has no unit tests in this diff, so a focus-reassignment regression would be silent.

The focused-pane bridge routing (chatBridge) plus ChatPaneContext registration/unregistration are the load-bearing correctness core of the split and have zero tests in this diff (tests deferred to phase 8), leaving the highest-risk paths unverified.

data-testid "chat-right-panel" (and chat-split-btn / conversation-find-toggle-btn / chat-open-in-new-window / chat-pane-close via headerControls) is rendered once per pane, so in a >=2-pane split these ids are non-unique and any e2e selector keyed on them will be strict-mode-ambiguous unless scoped under chat-pane-N.

SplitChatView, ChatPaneContext, and OpenInNewWindowAction are registered as kind:'via' with no standalone gallery story, so the runtime-health gate (console-error/contrast/a11y-name checks) never exercises the split view or the in-pane slide-over — those surfaces get zero automated a11y/runtime coverage.

SplitDivider attaches window pointermove/pointerup listeners on pointerdown and only removes them on pointerup, with no unmount cleanup effect, so a pane/divider that unmounts mid-drag (e.g. closePane during resize) leaks listeners bound to a stale closure — an untested edge path.

The web openConversationWindow accepts _opts.title but never uses it (underscore-prefixed, discarded), so the conversation title passed by OpenInNewWindowAction is dropped on web — the opened tab keeps the generic route title while the desktop path applies a title, an inconsistent user-visible result.

The desktop pop-out window falls back to the hardcoded generic title 'Conversation' when no title is passed, whereas the web path derives it from the page; using the actual conversation title consistently would make the OS window/taskbar entry identifiable.

New user-facing strings ('Open in split view', 'Close pane', 'Resize panes', 'Open in new window'/'Open in new tab', 'Close panel') are clear, consistently sentence-cased, and platform-adaptive where needed — no copy defects found in the new labels themselves.
per-pane composer store binding (pane?.store ?? Stores.Chat) reviewed: unconditional hook, sound single-pane fallback, consistent redirection — no defect
as typeof Stores.Chat cast on the per-pane store is currently sound (same store def) but could mask a future shape divergence
disposition: accepted: matches the established Stores.Chat bridge-type pattern used across the pane migration

finding: seedConversation used node fetch() instead of page.request.post like the sibling specs
three conversation-seeding idioms across the new specs (mkConv / seedConversation / inline)
disposition: accepted: converged scroll->page.request.post; remaining variance is per-spec-local and readable

finding: reload width assertion could false-green if persistence silently reset to default and the resize moved < tolerance; must prove divergence from default before reload
inline page.request.post seed lacked a status check (opaque undefined-id nav on failure)
pane-B-idle negative is a single point-in-time check right after pane A completes
disposition: accepted: pane A reply is fully awaited first, so a later cross-bleed frame is not a realistic path

finding: closePane focus assertion used === a || === c (under-tight); impl deterministically reassigns to panes[idx] === c
reorderPanes toIndex out-of-bounds branch untested (only fromIndex was)
